title: AI History — Technical Turning Points
key_insight: Each AI breakthrough solved a specific technical bottleneck. The current era was unlocked by three simultaneous advances: GPUs, big data, and backpropagation at scale.
body:
## Why the Current Wave is Different

Previous AI waves failed because of three bottlenecks:
1. Not enough compute
2. Not enough data
3. Training algorithms that didn't scale

All three were solved around 2010–2015.

## The Key Technical Breakthroughs

### 1943 — McCulloch-Pitts Neuron
First mathematical model of a neuron. Inspired by biology. Very simple: weighted inputs + threshold.

### 1958 — Perceptron (Frank Rosenblatt)
First learning algorithm: adjust weights based on errors. The computer learned from examples — a revolutionary concept.

### 1969 — Minsky & Papert Kill the Perceptron
Showed a perceptron can't learn XOR (a simple function). Funding collapsed. First AI winter.

### 1986 — Backpropagation (Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams)
An algorithm to train multi-layer networks. Solved the problem that killed the perceptron. But computers were still too slow to use it effectively.

### 2006 — Deep Belief Networks (Hinton)
Hinton showed how to train deep networks effectively using pretraining. Reignited interest in neural networks.

### 2012 — AlexNet + ImageNet
```
ImageNet: 1 million labelled images, 1000 categories
AlexNet: 8-layer CNN trained on 2 GPUs
Result: 15.3% error rate vs 26.2% (prior best)
Impact: 10.9 percentage point gap proved deep learning's dominance
```

### 2017 — Attention Is All You Need
The Transformer architecture. Self-attention replaces RNNs. Processes sequences in parallel → much faster to train. Became the backbone of every modern language model.

### 2020 — GPT-3 (175B parameters)
In-context learning: the model can learn tasks from examples in its prompt, without any weight updates. Changed what 'using AI' means.

## The Scaling Law Insight

Kaplan et al. (OpenAI, 2020) showed:

```
Model performance improves predictably with:
  - Model size (parameters)
  - Dataset size (tokens)
  - Compute (FLOPs)

This is called the 'neural scaling laws'
→ If you can afford more compute + data, you will get a better model
```
common_mistakes: Thinking backpropagation was invented in 2012 — it was invented in 1986, but lacked compute to be useful
Attributing ImageNet success only to the algorithm — the GPU training of Alex Krizhevsky was equally important
Thinking attention was invented with Transformers — additive attention (Bahdanau) predated Transformers by 3 years
mini_exercise: The Transformer paper is titled 'Attention Is All You Need'. What did it replace, and why was the replacement important for training speed? (Hint: look up 'RNN parallelisation problem')
real_world_usage: NVIDIA's stock went from $20 in 2015 to $900+ in 2024, almost entirely driven by GPU demand for AI training. The compute constraint that caused AI winters is now so monetised it's worth hundreds of billions of dollars. Understanding that compute = AI progress is now essential business knowledge.
explain_back_prompt: Explain why backpropagation didn't lead to practical AI in 1986 but did in 2012. What changed? List at least two factors.

build_task.title: Read the original AlexNet paper abstract
build_task.context: Reading primary sources is how engineers stay close to ground truth.
steps: Search: 'AlexNet ImageNet classification deep convolutional neural networks Krizhevsky'
Read just the abstract and introduction (2-3 pages)
Write down: what architecture did they use? What result did they achieve?
Find one thing in the paper that surprised you
build_task.expected_output: A summary of AlexNet's architecture, results, and one surprising finding.
build_task.hint: AlexNet used ReLU activation, dropout regularisation, and data augmentation — all of which became standard practice for reasons they explain.
